content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Ivan Altamirano 
Mayor Pro Tern 
City of Commerce 
2566 Kurt Ave. 
Commerce, CA 90040 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-19-092 
Dear Mr. Altamirano: 
June 11, 2019 
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of 
the Political Reform Act ("Act") 1 and Government Code Section 1090.2 Please note that we are 
only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and Section 1090, not 
under other conflict of interest prohibitions such as the common law or Public Contract Code. 
Also, note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 
FPPC Ops. 71 ); any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not 
the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for 
additional advice. 
Regarding our advice on Section 1090, we are required to forward your request and all 
pertinent facts relating to the request to the Attorney General's Office and the Los Angeles County 
District Attorney's Office, which we have done. (Section 1097.l(c)(3).) We did not receive a 
written response from either entity. (Section 1097. l(c)(4).) We are also required to advise you that, 
for purposes of Section 1090, the following advice "is not admissible in a criminal proceeding 
against any individual other than the requestor." (Section 1097.l(c)(5).) 
QUESTION 
Are the conflict of interest provisions of the Act or Section 1090 triggered if your spouse 
applies for the executive director position of a joint powers authority ("JP A") that you formally 
represented as an alternate board member? 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 
2 Sections 1090-1097.5. 


CONCLUSION 
File No. A-19-092 
Page No. 2 
No. Under the facts you have presented, neither the Act nor Section 1090 would prohibit 
your spouse from accepting the executive director position because you did not participate in any 
governmental decision or governmental contracting regarding your spouse's application. 
FACTS 
Your spouse is considering applying for the executive director position of a joint powers 
authority ("JPA") in Los Angeles County. The JPA is governed by a seven-member board from 
various cities. Each city provides one councilmember to represent them on the JP A board, as well 
as an alternate. 
You are a Councilmember from one of the cities that comprise the board. Currently, you do 
not serve on the board, either directly or as an alternate. You previously served as an alternate on 
the board but never attended a meeting. 
The City Council receives updates on the activities of the JPA and its board. However, only 
the JP A board members or alternates would have dealings with the negotiation or drafting of an 
employment contract or vote for executive director. That is, only the representative councilmember 
or the alternate would be involved in the decision making. Neither you nor the City Council as a 
whole would participate or affect the appointment of an executive director to the JP A. 
ANALYSIS 
The Act: 
The Act only prohibits financial conflicts of.interest in governmental decision-making. 
Section 87100 provides that no public official may make, participate in making, or in any way 
attempt to use his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official 
knows, or has reason to know, that he or she has a financial interest. Section 87103 defines a 
"financial interest" as follows: 
"A public official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of Section 87100 
if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, 
distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a member of his or her 
immediate family,3 or on any of the following: 
"(a) Any business entity in which the public official has a direct or indirect investment 
worth two thousand dollars ($2;000) or more. 
3 A public official always has an interest in his or her personal finances. A governmental decision will have an 
effect on this interest if the decision will result in the personal expenses, income, assets, or liabilities of the official or 
his or her immediate family increasing or decreasing. The financial effect is material if the official or the official's 
immediate family member will receive a measurable financial benefit or loss from the decision. (Regulation 18702.5) 


File No. A-19-092 
Page No. 3 
"(b) Any real property in which the public official has a direct or indirect interest worth two 
thousand dollars ($2,000) or more. 
"( c) Any source of income, except gifts or loans by a commercial lending institutioµ made 
in the regular course of business on terms available to the public without regard to official 
status, aggregating five hundred dollars ($500) or more in value provided or promised to, 
received by, the public official within 12 months prior to the time when the decision is 
made.4 
"( d) Any business entity in which the public official is a director; officer, partner, trustee, 
employee, or holds any position of management. 
"( e) Any donor of, or any intermediary or agent for a donor of, a gift or gifts aggregating 
[$470]5 or more in value provided to, received by, or promised to the public official within 
12 months prior to the time when the decision is made." 
Since the Act only prohibits the financial conflicts of interest enumerated above, you do not 
have a disqualifying conflict of interest solely by the fact that your spouse may apply for an 
executive position with a JP A on which you previously served as an alternate board member. 
Accordingly, you do not have a disqualifying conflict of interest under the Act with regards to your 
spouse applying to the JP A. 
Section 1090: 
Section 1090 provides, in part, that "[m]embers of the Legislature, state, county, district, 
judicial district, and city officers or employees shall not be financially interested in any contract 
made by them in their official capacity, or by any body or board of which they are members." 
Section 1090 is concerned with financial interests, other than remote or minimal interests, 
that prevent public officials from exercising absolute loyalty and undivided allegiance in furthering 
the best interests of their agencies. (Stigall v. Taft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565, 569.). Under Sectjon 1090, 
"the prohibited act is the making of a contract in which the official has a financial interest." (People 
v. Honig (1996) 48 Cal.App.4th 289, 333.) A contract that violates Section 1090 is void. (Thomson 
v. Call (1985) 38 Cal.3d 633, 646.) 
You have not presented facts indicating your involvement in the making or participating in 
making of a contract. Further, you have indicated that, in your official capacity, you will not 
participate or influence the JP A's decision to appoint an executive director. Only the representative 
from the City Council or his or her alternate is in position to vote on the executive director 
appointment. The City Council's involvement with the JPA is limited to receiving updates 
• 
4 Please note that salary from a governmental agency is not considered income under the Act. (Section 
82030(b)(2).) 
5 The amount of the value of gifts specified by this subdivision shall be adjusted biennially by the Commission 
to equal the same amount determined by the Commission pursuant to subdivision (t) of Section 89503. The quotation 
above has been modified with the current figure. 
,, 


File No. A-19-092 
Page No. 4 
regarding the activities of the JPA. Accordingly, Section 1090's prohibition is not applicable 
because you will not be making or influencing the making of a contract. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
RPOC:aja 
Sincerely, 
Dave Bainbridge 
General Counsel 
~ o~f..r 
By: 
Ryan O'Connor 
Courisel, Legal Division

QUESTION: Are the conflict of interest provisions of the Act or Section 1090 triggered if your spouse 
applies for the executive director position of a joint powers authority ("JP A") that you formally 
represented as an alternate board member?2 Sections 1090-1097.5.

CONCLUSION: No. Under the facts you have presented, neither the Act nor Section 1090 would prohibit 
your spouse from accepting the executive director position because you did not participate in any 
governmental decision or governmental contracting regarding your spouse's application.
